reason: The item targets a coherent engineering judgment (pressure/thermal weakening plus vent-path impairment vs. admissible breach modes) and has meaningful expert discrimination. However, several options introduce avoidable absolute or comparative claims (“more likely,” “always,” “same”) that are not tightly bounded by the stem, creating boundary ambiguity and reducing single-fault decoy purity.

The item cleanly targets two specific diagnostic failures: misidentifying the dominant heat-generation pathway at 100% SOC given an explicit 3:1 external/internal heat ratio, and overlooking the mechanical inevitability of pressure-driven breach when venting is constrained while casing strength collapses above 600°C. It strongly separates experts who respect quantitative boundary conditions (heat partition + burst pressure vs internal pressure) from those who rely on intuitive “internal chemistry dominates” and “containment reduces heat” heuristics, with minimal room for alternate interpretations beyond minor ambiguity about whether the second error is primarily heat-accounting or mechanical-risk framing.
